data.title: US Inventory Of Unsold US Homes At 3.5 Months Supply
The Man Who Bought Fire Island — WSJ
data.description:
By Candace Taylor | Photography by Ryan Lowry for WSJ. Magazine

Tristan Schukraft almost didn't go to Fire Island.

A California native, the serial tech entrepreneur was in New York City on a business trip about 12 years ago when friends invited him to part of the island known as the Pines, a famous gay vacation destination on a sliver of sand off Long Island.

Schukraft was skeptical. "My friend hit me up and said, 'You should come to Fire Island — you can sleep on the sofa,' " he recalls. "I was like, 'That's not really my thing.' " The jet-setting tech founder, now 45, prefers luxury hotels to the cramped "share houses" where many Fire Island vacationers stay. "You're in this house with a bunch of people you don't know — it's so awkward to me."

But he went, borrowing cash for the 30-minute ferry ride — "No one told me you need cash for the ferry!" — and ended up loving the wooded island, where cars are prohibited but deer and scantily clad men roam.

Still, Schukraft, based in Los Angeles and then Puerto Rico, made only one other short day trip to the Pines over the next 10 years. So in May 2024, he raised eyebrows by announcing a deal to pay about $17 million for the vast majority of the Pines commercial district, including a hotel, restaurant and nightclub and the iconic Blue Whale bar, where the Pines' famous "tea dance" party was created for gay singles in the 1960s.

Schukraft sees himself as a cultural preservationist, and the Pines is one part of an ambitious project to protect gay history and restore glory to landmark gay institutions. In April 2024, he paid $45 million for the Abbey Food & Bar, the inspiration for Chappell Roan's song "Pink Pony Club" and an icon of gay life in West Hollywood that had in recent years been beset by a series of complaints that patrons were drugged there. The problem is widespread and not unique to the Abbey, Schukraft says; the bar provides free test strips to customers and does staff trainings focused on the issue.

Schukraft is also reopening the shuttered nightclub Circo in Puerto Rico, and he bought the D.S. Tequila Co. nightclub in Chicago's Boystown neighborhood.

Some of these efforts have been met with skepticism, but none more than Schukraft's acquisition on Fire Island. The Pines — and the nearby community of Cherry Grove — are some of the gay community's most sacred spaces. For decades, the island's secluded location allowed gay people a temporary escape from their otherwise closeted lives. Its politically engaged, vocal residents are fiercely protective of the area's history and traditions, such as the tea dance and the annual Fourth of July drag queen "Invasion."

"We have a very opinionated, critical community who all have something to say," says Bobby Bonanno, founder of the Fire Island Pines Historical Society. Former commercial district owner Ian Reisner learned that the hard way in 2015, when he faced a boycott after he hosted a Manhattan event with same-sex-marriage opponent Ted Cruz.

Fire Island's status as a haven for the gay community is particularly crucial in the current political moment, Bonanno says. "You can be who you want, you can be with who you want — in these political times that becomes even more important."

Schukraft knows his plans are under a microscope. "I think we're going to bring a breath of fresh air into Fire Island — I think people are really going to like it," he says. After all, he has overcome adversity in business before. "I'm in sales," he says, "so no means yes."

Then again, Schukraft is not only a newcomer to the Pines, but has little experience running hotels or nightclubs. The founder of tech companies including the HIV telemedicine platform Mistr and the e-ticketing company ID90.com, Schukraft is now staging an audacious foray into the hospitality industry. On Fire Island, he will soon open an outpost of the first gay luxury hotel brand, Tryst Hotels, with other locations launching in Puerto Vallarta, Mexico, and San Juan, Puerto Rico. This series of rapid-fire acquisitions — focused on some of the most iconic institutions of gay life — have earned him the nickname "The CEO of Everything Gay."

On a recent afternoon at his new Tryst Puerto Vallarta hotel, a confident Schukraft looks the part in Hermès slip-ons, no-show socks and a crisp, pale-blue button-down. He wouldn't specify his net worth but says he's willing to spend big to ensure the precarious survival of beloved institutions like the Abbey and the Pines.

"Right now in the community, you have all these forefathers that are retiring," he says. "If somebody doesn't step in and take over, they're going to disappear."

Techno music thumps through the open-air restaurant at the Tryst Puerto Vallarta, where Schukraft is sampling new menu items. The hotel is in the last stages of construction and is scheduled to officially open this March, but the restaurant has already started hosting Drag Brunch on Sundays; attendees receive hot-pink fans with the Tryst logo on them.

Tanned and fit with sandy hair, Schukraft is describing his work-hard, play-hard lifestyle. "On Friday I'm exhausted from the workweek, but on Sunday I'm even more exhausted from partying all weekend," he says.

In some ways, he doesn't fit the mold of a nightlife impresario. "What is the definition of a crepe?" he asks the server who places a plate of chipotle chicken crepes on the table in front of him. When it comes to dining, Schukraft's tastes run mostly to calorie counting. "I'm a big vodka sugar-free Red Bull fan — that's my drink of choice," he says, tentatively sipping the hotel's blackberry martini, which is garnished with gold-coated blackberries. "But I'm trying to venture out."

He doesn't like gin or whiskey, and too many margaritas irritate his throat, but he does drink vodka with diet cranberry juice. "No bar ever serves diet cranberry — I don't get it," he says. "But it's something we're going to do here. That's one of the things about all my properties. I'm really trying to put my personal touch on them — so things I like, which hopefully other people like."

At the Tryst Puerto Vallarta, that means a palette of whites and neutrals with the occasional splash of pink, like the salmon-colored cushions on the cafe chairs. The new Tryst hotel in the Pines, slated to open in mid-May, will have a similar look, as will the Tryst San Juan, which will soon undergo renovation. Schukraft also hopes to launch the Tryst Ipanema in Brazil sometime in the next few years.

Schukraft grew up outside Los Angeles in a family of airline-industry employees — his father was a top executive at Northwest — and earned pocket money by returning luggage carts at the airport for 25 cents each. "We made like $30 and thought that was a lot of money," he recalls.

He spent much of his time at LAX, where at 17 he was discovered by Boss Model Management. He moved to Europe to pursue modeling, missing his last year of high school and taking his GED. "I don't know why my parents said yes," he says. Soon after that, he told them he was gay in a tearful scene in the family's garage. "My dad thought I was going to be held back in life career-wise. My mother thought I was going to get AIDS and die," Schukraft remembers. "My parents were very liberal, but it's different when it's your son."

Still, they were supportive, as were his two brothers and the rest of his extended family. "Everybody had to accept it or deal with my mother, " he says.

Modeling soon gave way to various business ventures, such as selling Razor scooters at local swap meets. In the early 2000s, Schukraft hit on a winning idea with ID90, a travel-booking website for airline personnel.

ID90 later evolved into a platform for e-ticketing — still new in the early 2000s. "Here I am, I'm this young kid trying to tell [airlines] how to run their business," Schukraft recalls. Because his technology would allow airlines to hire fewer employees, "I was not very well liked in the airline industry. People were like, 'Why do we need e-tickets? We have paper tickets.' It was so tough." When Hawaiian Airlines eventually became his first client, "I was on fumes," he says. "I've never been happier to fly to Hawaii." Other airlines started signing on, and the company soon had 111 employees around the world.

After 14 years of constant travel — "I know every airport code there is" — Schukraft wanted a break. He stepped back from ID90, although he and his parents still own a 30 percent stake in the company, and moved to West Hollywood. After a month, he was bored. So he ran an unsuccessful campaign for West Hollywood City Council and invested in the gay magazine Frontiers. His next big hit was Mistr, which launched shortly before Covid, becoming the largest telemedicine platform for HIV prevention and care. Today it serves half a million patients, Schukraft says, adding that he has also invested in real estate and other ventures.

His abrupt pivot into hotels and nightclubs is partly altruistic, Schukraft says. After moving to Puerto Rico with his partner, Melvin Alexander, during the pandemic, Schukraft discovered that Hurricane Maria — and Circo's closing — had decimated the island's gay nightlife. "One gay institution leaves, and then another one leaves, and before you know it, there's no gay community there," he says. To revive it, he bought Circo and the Tryst San Juan hotel. "My idea is, Build it, and they will come," he says.

The move also makes business sense, Schukraft says: Owning gay bars and hotels is a great way to promote Mistr. Free for users, Mistr makes its money through fees paid by nonprofits via a government program that subsidizes the cost of HIV prevention drugs; the company may soon face challenges to its business model as Congress considers changes to the program.

Schukraft also sees a void in the marketplace for uber-luxury gay hotels. While there are some hotel chains aimed at gay customers, Schukraft envisions a higher level of service. "There are gays that want the five-star experience where they can be true and authentic to themselves," he says. "Why do we have to settle for a four-star?" In the past, investors have shied away from the niche, arguing that there aren't enough wealthy gay customers. "I'm debunking the myth," he says, "that the gay market's not big enough."

Until recently, Schukraft and Alexander were part of a well-publicized throuple. On social media Schukraft posted images of the three posing in Speedos. But the trio broke up this past summer, and over the holidays Schukraft proposed to Alexander, with his family looking on, in what they believe to be the first gay marriage proposal at the South Pole. "He's the center of my world, why not the center of the world, right?" Schukraft says.

The glamorous lifestyle Schukraft documents on Instagram, along with his status as a Pines newcomer, has led to a certain amount of consternation among Fire Island residents, who fear his changes won't reflect the Pines' laid-back, barefoot vibe and storied history. The tea dance, for example, was created at a time when men weren't allowed to dance together in public; line dances such as the Hully Gully, with a sprinkling of women here and there, allowed them to sidestep that rule.

Schukraft "has no history there whatsoever," Bonanno says. "My concern is paying attention to the past and respecting what has come before. When people go to the Pines," he adds, "they're not looking for Manhattan skyscrapers and metal. They're looking for a beach aesthetic. You've got to honor that."

The Pines is also a notoriously difficult and expensive place to build. Materials must be delivered by boat, contractors can charge what they want, and the island is extremely seasonal. Virtually deserted between September and May, it leaves businesses a slim three-month window to make a profit. "It's a very punishing environment," says Andrew Kirtzman, another former owner of the Pines commercial district.

Schukraft is focused on the ways he can run Pines businesses better than in the past. For starters, he wants to rebrand the entire district. " 'Commercial district' is like 'public storage' to me," he says. "It does not sound sexy whatsoever. We're going to rename it 'the Waterfront.' "

Everyone acknowledges, meanwhile, that the buildings were badly in need of renovation when he bought them. The 1960s-era cinderblock hotel with shared bathrooms, nicknamed The Botel, "was an eyesore," Kirtzman says. It was condemned two years ago and hasn't opened since.

When renovations are completed, the 30-room hotel will have not only private bathrooms, but an elevator. Meanwhile, Schukraft is revamping the menu at the Canteen restaurant, banishing the "frozen cafeteria food" it had become known for.

"With very little effort we'll make more money this year," Schukraft says. "Just having a place to eat is going to have a huge impact."

Schukraft has long prided himself on his ability to move quickly. In all his businesses, he "just wants everything done yesterday," says his friend Karen Bauer. But that has already led to frustration in the Pines, a tiny village where "no one gets up before 11 a.m.," says Scott Bromley of Bromley Caldari Architects, a longtime Pines homeowner who is working with Schukraft on his plans for the commercial district. "Things don't just get done by snapping your fingers or if you have a lot of money. It's pedantic and a little slow-moving at times."

After making his Pines acquisition, for example, Schukraft had to wait months longer than expected for the liquor license to transfer over.

"I'm not sure it's going to all get built by this next season," says Bromley. "They have great ideas and they do a great job, but they just don't know how it really works on Fire Island."

Another open question is if Schukraft can make the Pines — and his other hospitality ventures — profitable, given the amount of money he is pouring into them. He declined to specify how much the Pines project will cost, but said he is bankrolling it himself and is spending $3 million on staff housing alone: He is revamping "the ranch," two small buildings where Pines workers previously lived in tiny rooms with bunk beds and shared bathrooms. Among his improvements: Wi-Fi and TVs in the rooms. "A lot of people think I'm crazy, it's not necessary," he says. "But if you treat your employees well, they work harder for you."

Still, Schukraft freely admits that he may never recoup his investment. "Serving the gay community is a passion project," he says. It's more about leaving a legacy than maximizing profits.

"Someone said to me, 'If you have to put in an extra $1 million to make this perfect, you will.' That is my personality," Schukraft says. "At the end of the day, my name is on it."

He takes a tiny bite of "Tryst Leches" cake, made from a friend's mother's recipe. "I recognize that you can't make everyone happy," he says. "When you try to make everybody happy, you make no one happy."

Write to Candace Taylor at candace.taylor@wsj.com

data.title: Palantir Stock Rises After 2-Day Slump. Where Wall Street Thinks It Goes Next. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Callum Keown

Friday looks set to be a crucial day for Palantir Technologies stock after its two-day 15% slump as investors assess whether the weakness is a blip, or something more sustained.

The shares are pointing 1% higher ahead of the open so the damage seems to have paused for now. It's still the third-best performer in the S&P 500 this year, climbing 40% so far in 2025.

Two things in particular spooked investors earlier this week — CEO Alex Karp's new plan to sell up to $1.2 billion worth of shares and reports that the Trump administration has warned the U.S. Department of Defense about budget cuts. The data analytics company has several military contracts for the use of its artificial intelligence technology.

Both of those things, in the context of Palantir's impressive rally and lofty valuation, have caused concern and raised questions over whether a period of prolonged weakness lies ahead for the stock.

While a third consecutive day of losses does not mean it's the start of a broader slump, investors would certainly hope to see the streak end and some stability return.

Wall Street thinks the stock has further to fall — analysts covering the shares have an average price target of $95.28, implying 10% downside to Thursday's closing price. Just 31% have Buy ratings on the stock, with 50% saying Hold and 19% recommending Sell.

The shares are expensive, trading at 190 times future earnings after jumping almost nineteen-fold since the end of 2022.

William Blair analysts led by Louie DiPalma reiterated their Underperform rating on the stock late Thursday. Palantir's U.S. government business experienced a pronounced revenue deceleration in 2021 and 2022 and DiPalma said "there is high risk of this happening again."

"A sharp revenue growth deceleration would likely result in valuation multiple contraction," he said, adding that William Blair sees greater than 20% downside to the shares over the next few years.

Barron's argued it may be time to sell Palantir stock earlier this week, before the slump, citing the rally and high valuation. The reasons behind that call are still in play.

Write to Callum Keown at callum.keown@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: The Dow Jones Index Opens 0.82% Lower
data.description:
In New York, the Dow Jones Index is losing 361 points.

Losses were led by UnitedHealth (-9.51%), Walmart (-2.01%) and Salesforce (-1.40%).

Offsetting the fall, top gainers were Walt Disney (0.93%), Coca-Cola (0.69%) and J&J (0.47%).

data.title: January US Existing Home Sales Fall More Than Expected, Still Up From Year Earlier
data.description:
The pace of US existing home sales fell by 4.9% to a 4.08 million seasonally adjusted annual rate in January from 4.29 million in December, compared with a smaller expected decrease to a 4.13 million rate in a survey compiled by Bloomberg as of 7:35 am ET, data from the National Association of Realtors released Friday showed.

Total sales were up 2% from a year earlier.

"Mortgage rates have refused to budge for several months despite multiple rounds of short-term interest rate cuts by the Federal Reserve," National Association of Realtors Chief Economist Lawrence Yun said. "When combined with elevated home prices, housing affordability remains a major challenge."

Sales of single-family homes were down 5.2%, while condominium sales decreased by 2.4%. Sales were down in three of the four regions of the country compared with the previous month, while they increased in three out of four regions from a year earlier.

Homes remained on the market an average of 41 days, above 35 days in December and 36 days a year ago and the highest since prior to the pandemic, NAR said.

The supply of homes for sale increased to 1.18 million homes in January from 1.14 million in December and were up 16.8% from the 1.01 million level a year ago.

"More housing supply allows strongly qualified buyers to enter the market," Yun said. "But for many consumers, both increased inventory and lower mortgage rates are necessary for them to purchase a different home or become first-time homeowners."

The month supply on market increased to 3.5 months from 3.2 months in December and was higher than the 3.0 months supply a year ago.

The median home price declined to $396,900 from $403,700 level in December but was up 4.8% from $378,600 level one year ago.

The monthly existing home sales report from the National Association of Realtors measures sales of single-family and multi-family homes for resale at the time of closing, including the number of existing homes available and the median sales price. A strong reading is a positive sign for mortgage lenders and related consumer product companies

data.title: Waste Plastic Upcycling to Delist From Euronext Growth Oslo
data.description:
WPU - Waste Plastic Upcycling's shares will be delisted from Euronext Growth Oslo on Feb. 25, according to a Friday release.

The delisting, which was filed earlier in the week, follows the completion of Vitol's takeover of the green cleantech company.

data.title: Update: Market Chatter: US Department of Justice, SEC Probing CrowdStrike-Carahsoft Deal
data.description:
(Updates with CrowdStrike's response in the fourth paragraph.)

The US Department of Justice and the Securities and Exchange Commission are investigating a $32 million deal between CrowdStrike Holdings and Carahsoft to provide cybersecurity tools to the Internal Revenue Service, Bloomberg reported Friday, citing two people familiar with the matter and a document it saw.

Investigators have been interviewing people and gathering records related to the deal, the report said.

Carahsoft paid CrowdStrike for the deal, which the cybersecurity firm completed in 2023, but the IRS never bought the products, Bloomberg first reported in October.

"We stand by the accounting of the transaction," a CrowdStrike spokesperson told MT Newswires.

Carahsoft did not immediately reply to a request for comment from MT Newswires.

(Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.)

data.title: Guardant Health Is Maintained at Buy by Stifel
NexPoint Urges Postponement of UDF IV-Ready Capital Merger Vote, Readies Competing Proposal
data.description:
NexPoint Diversified Real Estate Trust on Friday urged the board of United Development Funding IV, or UDF IV, to postpone the special shareholders meeting to vote on its proposed merger with Ready Capital , as it is preparing a competing proposal.

NexPoint said its concerns about the proposed merger include the risk of further erosion of Ready Capital's stock price, which is a significant percentage of the consideration for the deal. The investment trust also said that the proposed merger provides benefits to the UDF IV board and management "that do not reflect the interest of UDF IV shareholders."

NexPoint requested a 30-day postponement of the special meeting to April 4, in order for the UDF IV board to be able to adequately review a competing proposal it is putting together. Even with the postponement, the proposed merger could still be completed by April 15, the deadline under the merger agreement, the investment trust said.

NexPoint, Ready Capital, and UDF IV did not immediately reply to a request for comment from MT Newswires.

NexPoint Diversified shares rose 0.6% in recent trading, while Ready Capital was up 0.8%.

data.title: Salesforce's Q4 Results Likely to Top 'Low Bar,' Oppenheimer Says
data.description:
Salesforce is in a slightly stronger "operating environment" in Q4 with stock valuation at reasonable levels, given its growth prospects and cash generation, Oppenheimer said in an earnings preview Friday.

The cloud-based software company is expected to deliver positive Q4 results that should "exceed a low bar" and constructive updates on Agentforce, although guidance may fall short of some investors' estimates, Oppenheimer said. The company is due to report Q4 results on Wednesday.

Oppenheimer analysts said they project revenue of about $10 billion for Q4 and predict $2.60 in earnings per share for the quarter.

Investor sentiment on Salesforce turned more cautious this year due to a sales leadership transition that may affect the company's go-to-market approach in 2025, as well as the uncertain timing of revenue from Agentforce, the investment firm said.

The upcoming departure of Salesforce's president and COO was unexpected, raising worries about possible sales restructuring challenges through 2025, according to the note.

Salesforce can regain investor confidence by delivering above-consensus results, offering clear updates on Agentforce and Data Cloud, setting new medium-term financial targets, and benefiting from a possible IT spending recovery, Oppenheimer analysts said.

Oppenheimer maintained an outperform rating and $415 price target on Salesforce.

data.title: Update: Ford to Initiate Recall of Over 240,000 Cars in US Due to Seat Belt Problems
data.description:
(Updates to use the statement from the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration throughout.)

Ford Motor is recalling over 240,000 cars in the US because of possible problems with their seat belts, according to the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration or NHTSA.

Reuters reported the recall in a Friday news brief.

The issue involves specific 2020-2021 Ford Explorer and Lincoln Aviator models where the anchor bolts for the seat belt buckles may be "improperly secured," according to the statement. The NHTSA said dealers will inspect the vehicles' seat belt buckle anchor bolts and affected components will be replaced free of charge.

data.title: UBS Downgrades TFI International to Neutral From Buy, Adjusts PT to $107 From $163
AlphaValue/Baader Europe Lifts Lloyds Banking Group Forecasts
data.description:
AlphaValue/Baader Europe raised its earnings forecasts for Lloyds Banking Group , reflecting a more optimistic outlook based on the UK-based lender's updated 2025 guidance.

Full-year 2025 and 2026 EPS are now projected to reach 0.0771 pound sterling and 0.0782 pound, up 9.95% and 2.99%, respectively, according to a Thursday note.

"This is attributable to a combination of increased revenue expectations (+2.5% in 2025 equally driven by interest and non-interest income), better cost control (operating expense projections reduced by 3% in 2025) and lower loan impairments (-16% in 2025)," analysts said. They, however, warned that the forecast does not take into account potential provisions related to motor finance.

The research firm has a reduce rating on Lloyds, with a target price of 0.635 pound.

data.title: Truist Securities Adjusts Churchill Downs Price Target to $162 From $165, Maintains Buy Rating
data.description: Churchill Downs has an average rating of Buy and mean price target of $160.44, according to analysts polled by FactSet.
